children.name: military and service
So when things final cooled down one guy—American, our troops—he was going out just to be a listening post at the edge of the wood line, because we had our dead troops there.
children.context: So, while we was firing up a bunch of ammunition into the woods where the enemy was supposed to be, and the artillery dropping around us pretty close but not on us, the enemy knows the closer they are to us, the safer they are from that artillery. So when things final cooled down one guy—American, our troops—he was going out just to be a listening post at the edge of the wood line, because we had our dead troops there. But then he got ambushed.

children.name: So when it finally cooled down they was not firing back at us, and we assumed that either they were gone or they were dead, the enemy.
children.context: So we start firing our guns and throwing grenades until finally killed him. So when it finally cooled down they was not firing back at us, and we assumed that either they were gone or they were dead, the enemy. So we had our helicopters call in and we had to load up our dead.

children.name: They don’t have this kind of information in books.
children.context: The looked at me almost like I was a ghost, because they couldn’t believe. They don’t have this kind of information in books. They couldn’t believe what they were hearing and I’m pretty sure they’ve seen—I did good today—my emotion was not as good then, but I got it together.

children.name: We never had any violence because we did not cross the line.
children.context: And then something else might have happened. We never had any violence because we did not cross the line. 	W:	When you say, “Cross the line” could you explain?

children.name: And they have very little in the books about the Vietnam War.
children.context: The lady Ms. Milton, Liz Milton, she teach history, and they were studying about the Vietnam War. And they have very little in the books about the Vietnam War. So I went and I talked, and I guess I don’t want to tell this story before I get to talking about Vietnam unless you want me to talk into it now, but I had to tell them how I lived in Vietnam.

children.name: This company had got hit pretty hard by the enemy and they didn’t have really enough to maintain so they rebuilt the fourth by adding more troops out there.
children.context: And after we was there for a while and got our jungle fatigues, and jungle boots, and all that kind of good stuff, and our weapon—which I had an M-16—they flew us out in the field and we joined another company. This company had got hit pretty hard by the enemy and they didn’t have really enough to maintain so they rebuilt the fourth by adding more troops out there. 	W:	So you were the replacements, but you became a part of that group?

children.name: So we had our helicopters call in and we had to load up our dead.
children.context: So when it finally cooled down they was not firing back at us, and we assumed that either they were gone or they were dead, the enemy. So we had our helicopters call in and we had to load up our dead. We always have gotten our dead out; not under fire.

children.name: We always have gotten our dead out; not under fire.
children.context: So we had our helicopters call in and we had to load up our dead. We always have gotten our dead out; not under fire. The helicopters come in and land, two of us get our buddy in.

children.name: You fight the Vietcong which can use booby traps and all that kind of stuff.
children.context: Another incident is somebody killed a bunch of—there’s two types of people you fight. You fight the Vietcong which can use booby traps and all that kind of stuff. We didn’t have to worry about booby traps, pungent pits, and North Vietnamese regiments.

children.name: We didn’t have to worry about booby traps, pungent pits, and North Vietnamese regiments.
children.context: You fight the Vietcong which can use booby traps and all that kind of stuff. We didn’t have to worry about booby traps, pungent pits, and North Vietnamese regiments. Now I was out in the jungle.

children.name: But anyway they had killed these Vietnamese, and normally that we don’t bury the enemy, but it was an area that the United States used; a convoy going through.
children.context: They got lightweight machine guns, they got motors, and they’re expert motormans. But anyway they had killed these Vietnamese, and normally that we don’t bury the enemy, but it was an area that the United States used; a convoy going through. And the dead was there for about a week.

children.name: And the dead was there for about a week.
children.context: But anyway they had killed these Vietnamese, and normally that we don’t bury the enemy, but it was an area that the United States used; a convoy going through. And the dead was there for about a week. Kinda like on the side of the road and in the woodland.

children.name: And sometimes when we would go out on AOP, which is a listening post, we might go out that night like phone in, go out from the main compound in case the enemy was trying to move at night, but we never did experienced the enemy moving at night.
children.context: We had air mattresses. And sometimes when we would go out on AOP, which is a listening post, we might go out that night like phone in, go out from the main compound in case the enemy was trying to move at night, but we never did experienced the enemy moving at night. 	W:	You think they were there though?

children.name: And then later on they brought some ammunition in and it kinda cooled down then.
children.context: We lost quite a few. And then later on they brought some ammunition in and it kinda cooled down then. Well before it cooled down it had got so bad that they called—they wouldn’t let the gunships come in which is the helicopter with rockets and machine gun fire.

children.name: I was just praying to myself that I hope all the enemy down there is dead and nobody’s gonna shoot me while—which they were dead, dead or either they had backed off.
children.context: C:	It was a good sign of peace. I was just praying to myself that I hope all the enemy down there is dead and nobody’s gonna shoot me while—which they were dead, dead or either they had backed off. So I was just so glad to get out.

children.name: The country today can be argued is much different in terms of how people look at, and respond to social issues than they did in the late 1960s.

The country today can be argued is much different in terms of how people look at, and respond to social issues than they did in the late 1960s. The late 1960s there was the Vietnam War, there was the movement, there was King, there was Johnson not running for election.

children.context: The country today can be argued is much different in terms of how people look at, and respond to social issues than they did in the late 1960s. children.name: And I ask that because many people I speak to from your generation tell me that something is missing today that used to exist.
children.context: I want to know in your opinion what is a difference or differences that you can point out between the type of education that you received at Central Academy, and what young people are receiving today? And I ask that because many people I speak to from your generation tell me that something is missing today that used to exist. I want to know if you might comment on that.

children.name: And I understand that you’re talking about segregation, but I was trying to get it spoken to in your own words.
children.context: W:	Could you talk about that difference? And I understand that you’re talking about segregation, but I was trying to get it spoken to in your own words. What was it like living in a segregated environment by law that you had to be separate?

children.name: That’s talking about in the [19]50s.
children.context: used to go out into the woods and cut down trees, and bring some wood back. That’s talking about in the [19]50s. When I went on to probably the sixth grade the block schools, which they had built, they were sixth, seventh and eighth.

children.name: protest on social issues where that doesn’t happen today.
children.context: People would go into the streets and March and protest on social issues where that doesn’t happen today. There’s the internet, people are satisfied with petitions and just sit and watch, and music—just like it was before, but a little bit different.

children.name: There’s the internet, people are satisfied with petitions and just sit and watch, and music—just like it was before, but a little bit different.

And from that we were able to produce a podcast which we shared with you and appreciate your feedback and participation on that.

children.name: W:	Alright we’re returning to our interview with Mr. Cornelius Clayton on September 9th, 2011 after a brief interlude, and we are ready to move from Vietnam, and talk about what happened when you got home; your career opportunities and your continuing education.
children.context: They couldn’t cross over. 	W:	Alright we’re returning to our interview with Mr. Cornelius Clayton on September 9th, 2011 after a brief interlude, and we are ready to move from Vietnam, and talk about what happened when you got home; your career opportunities and your continuing education. I’ll leave it to you to tell us what happened when you got back.

children.name: But I was able, and I will continue as long as I’m able if somebody ask me to speak, and tell what went down, because a lot of time what you see on TV is not what happened over there.
children.context: But they could see that I must have had a very bad traumatic experience. But I was able, and I will continue as long as I’m able if somebody ask me to speak, and tell what went down, because a lot of time what you see on TV is not what happened over there. A lot of stuff you see is just a dramatization and they hype it up.

children.name: I’ll leave it to you to tell us what happened when you got back.

I’ll leave it to you to tell us what happened when you got back. 	C:	Okay.

children.name: career and work and money
W:	How long did he operate his business?
children.context: But he owned his own business and he did well. 	W:	How long did he operate his business? 	C:	He operated his business.

children.context: Lincoln High School here in Gainesville. 	W:	Everybody had problems with Lincoln. Everybody tells me.

children.name: But he owned his own business and he did well.
children.context: Except my grandfather, he was a well driller and he owned his own business, that’s different from the stores. But he owned his own business and he did well. 	W:	How long did he operate his business?

children.name: C:	He operated his business.
children.context: W:	How long did he operate his business? 	C:	He operated his business. He used to go back and forth upon the season in New York, and I guess he got tired of that, and he decided to drill wells.

children.name: C:	Lincoln had some big boys, too.
children.context: Whether it’s Jerkins— 	C:	Lincoln had some big boys, too. So when you got some big boys you’re in and you don’t equal up, you don’t match up.

children.name: C:	His name was DC.
children.context: W:	And what was his name? 	C:	His name was DC. And I could vaguely see because when the round went off, you could see like a cloud of dust.
